actions.thought: I am holding 9d 2s in the Small Blind/Button, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Heads-Up No-Limit Hold'em (bottom 10-15%). While playing from the button is advantageous, and a wide opening range (often 80%+) is standard in heads-up play, 92o is simply too weak to profitably limp or raise. It has poor playability and very low equity against any reasonable defense range. Since we are 100 BB deep and this is the first hand, there is no need to make a hyper-aggressive play with trash. The standard GTO play here is to fold.

I am on the button with 6c 4c, which is a decent speculative hand in a heads-up situation. Raising is preferred over limping to take the initiative and potentially win the blinds immediately. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here to apply pressure on the Big Blind.

I am in the Big Blind with 4s 5h facing a 3x raise (300 chips) from the Button/Small Blind. In Heads-Up play, the Button acts as the Small Blind. The pot is currently 400, and I need to call 200 to see a flop, offering me pot odds of 33% (200/600). My hand, 4-5 offsuit, is one of the weaker hands in the deck, with poor playability out of position. Against a standard or wide opening range, 45o has roughly 32-35% raw equity, but realizing that equity out of position against a 3x raise is difficult due to domination and lack of suitedness. Given the larger-than-standard raise size (3bb vs 2bb) and the weakness of my hand, folding is the most profitable play.

I am in the Small Blind (Button) with T5o. At 100 BB deep in a Heads-Up match, I hold the positional advantage post-flop, but T5o is a very weak hand with poor playability and equity. While optimal strategy involves opening a wide range (80%+) from the button, T5o falls near the bottom of the range and is often a fold or a mixed strategy at best. Since it is early in the tournament (Hand 3), avoiding marginal spots with trash hands is prudent to preserve the stack.

In a heads-up situation, I should be playing the majority of my buttons. Although 5s 2s is a weak hand, it has some post-flop potential being suited. Since Pro has folded to every action so far in this tournament, I will put in a standard 3x raise to 300 to test if they will continue to play tight or defend their big blind.

I am holding 5h 2c, which is statistically one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em due to the lack of connectivity and low rank. The opponent (Flash) has raised to 300 (3BB) from the Button/SB. The pot is currently 400, and I have to call 200 more to see a flop. This gives me pot odds of 2:1 (33% equity required). Even in a heads-up scenario where ranges are wider, 52o has roughly 29-32% equity against a wide opening range and plays very poorly post-flop due to reverse implied odds and difficulty making strong pairs. Defending against a 3x raise with this hand is -EV. The correct play is to fold.

In Heads-Up play, K2s is a standard open from the Button (Small Blind). It falls well within the top 50-80% of hands typically played from this position. Since I have folded the first four hands, my image is currently very tight, which increases the likelihood of the opponent folding to a raise (stealing the blinds). Limping is an option, but given the opponent has shown aggression when on the button (3x opens), raising to 200 (min-raise) allows me to define the pot size, take the initiative, and play in position with a hand that has decent high-card and flush equity.

I have Ace-Queen offsuit in the Big Blind, which is a premium hand in heads-up play. My opponent, Pro, has raised from the Button to 200. Given the strength of my hand and the stack depths (nearly 100BB), a 3-bet is the standard play for value and to take control of the pot. I will raise to 600 total to build the pot and potentially end the hand pre-flop or set up a post-flop advantage.

I am holding K2s on the Button (Small Blind) in a heads-up match. I opened to 200 (2bb) and the opponent in the Big Blind 3-bet to 600 (6bb). While K2s is a reasonable hand to open from the button, it performs poorly against a 3-bet range. Although I would have position post-flop, the pot odds require me to call 400 to win a total pot of 1200, meaning I need roughly 33% equity realization. Against a standard or value-heavy 3-bet range (which is likely given my tight image from folding the first four hands), K2s is often dominated by better Kings or Aces and struggles to realize its equity. It is early in the tournament with deep stacks, so avoiding marginal spots where I am likely dominated is the prudent play.

Ace-four offsuit is a standard opening hand from the button in heads-up play. My opponent has shown a high frequency of folding to pre-flop aggression in previous hands (Hands 2, 4, and 5). I will continue to exploit this tendency by raising to 300.

I hold Th 5d in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise (to 300) from the Button. T5o is a very weak hand, disconnected and unsuited, making it difficult to play post-flop, especially out of position. Although the pot odds are roughly 3:1, my ability to realize equity with this hand is poor. The opponent has been aggressive, opening frequently, but defending this specific hand is a losing play in the long run. I fold to preserve my stack.

I am on the Button (Small Blind) in a Heads-Up match with a stack of roughly 93bb. My hand is 7d 6h, an off-suit connector. In Heads-Up play, the Button should open a very wide range of hands (often 80-100%). 76o is well within the playable range due to its ability to make straights and the positional advantage I hold post-flop. I have been playing extremely tightly so far, folding almost every hand, which may lead my opponent to believe I only play premiums. A min-raise to 200 puts pressure on the Big Blind and allows me to take the initiative. If I am 3-bet, I can easily fold this marginal hand, but if called, I can navigate post-flop in position.

K2o is a marginal hand, but getting 3:1 odds to call a min-raise in a heads-up match is mathematically correct. I need roughly 25% equity to call, and K2o against a standard small blind opening range typically has well over 40% equity. While I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, the pot odds justify seeing a flop.

I have flopped trips with a King kicker on a very dry board (2-2-9). I have a massive range advantage and the nut advantage here. The best play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) to allow them to continuation-bet with their entire range, including bluffs and weaker pairs. Leading out would likely fold out their air, whereas checking keeps their range wide and maximizes my potential value.

The flop comes 2h 2s 9c, an extremely dry and paired texture. As the pre-flop raiser, I retain the range advantage as I hold all strong overpairs (AA-TT) and strong 9s, while the Big Blind's calling range is capped. My hand (7 high) has effectively zero showdown value, so checking back likely forfeits the pot unless I hit a lucky turn. A small continuation bet (c-bet) is highly effective here because the opponent will miss this specific board a large percentage of the time. Given my tight image in previous hands, a bet is likely to be respected. I will bet approximately 1/3 of the pot to deny equity to random overcards and fold out total air without risking too many chips.

I have flopped trips with a King kicker on an extremely dry board (2h-2s-9c). Since there are virtually no draws and I have the board locked down, raising now would likely force my opponent to fold their bluffs and air. By calling the 150 bet, I keep Pro's entire range in and allow them to continue semi-bluffing or value-betting weaker hands on the turn. This maximizes my expected value by letting the opponent dictate the betting while I hold a near-nutted hand.

I have trips with a King kicker (Kd-2c) on a board of 2h-2s-9c-Ad. The Ace on the turn is a significant card because it likely hits the pre-flop raiser's (Pro) range. By checking here, I allow Pro to continue betting with their semi-bluffs or value bet with an Ace. Leading out might cause Pro to fold hands they would otherwise bluff with, whereas checking keeps their entire range in and maximizes the potential value I can extract from a monster hand.

The turn card is an Ace, which is the perfect scare card for my range as the pre-flop raiser. Given my extremely tight image in the first 6 hands, the opponent likely perceives my range as strong (Ax, high pairs). The opponent called a bet on a dry 2-2-9 flop, which typically indicates a mid-pocket pair (33-88) or a 9. The Ace puts significant pressure on that range. With 7-high, I have zero showdown value, so checking back essentially forfeits the pot. A second barrel here leverages the scary board texture and my image to generate fold equity against better hands. A bet of 400 (slightly over half pot) is sufficient to fold out pocket pairs and floats.

I have trips with a King kicker on a 2-2-9-A board. The Ace on the turn is a card that likely hits the pre-flop raiser's range, which makes it a great card for them to continue semi-bluffing or value betting. If I raise now, I risk folding out all of Pro's bluffs and only getting called by hands that are either beating me (like 99 or A2) or strong Aces. By calling, I keep the pot manageable and allow Pro to continue betting on the river with a wide range, maximizing my potential to extract value from a hand that is currently very under-represented.

I have trip deuces with a King kicker on a board of 2h-2s-9c-Ad-Jc. Since I have been check-calling the entire hand, my hand is very well disguised and looks like a weak Ace or a Nine. The Ace on the turn and the Jack on the river are cards that hit the pre-flop raiser's range significantly. By checking again, I allow Pro to continue their aggression with bluffs or value-bet their one-pair hands like AK, AQ, or AJ (which I beat). Leading out now would likely fold out their bluffs and only get called by hands that either beat me (Full Houses) or strong Aces. Checking is the best play to maximize value from Pro's wide range.

I hold 7-high and have fired two barrels on a board of 2-2-9-A. The opponent called my continuation bet on the flop and, more importantly, called my second barrel on the Ace turn. Calling a bet on the Ace turn against a tight opponent strongly suggests the opponent has at least an Ace or a very strong 9 and is unwilling to fold. The Jack on the river changes little; it unlikely improves my perceived range enough to force a fold from an Ace. Since the opponent's range for calling the turn is strong and likely inelastic to a river bluff, firing a third barrel has low expected value. I will check and surrender this pot to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with Q4o. While this is a marginal hand, I have a significant stack lead and have been successful in previous hands by applying pre-flop pressure. My opponent has folded to my raises in 3 out of the first 6 hands. I will maintain an aggressive image and put in a standard 3x open raise to 300 to attempt to take down the blinds immediately or play the hand in position if called.

I hold Pocket Nines (9d 9s) in the Big Blind. My opponent, Flash, has raised to 300 (3bb) from the Button. This is a strong starting hand in heads-up play, ranking well above the median winning hand. While a 3-bet for value and protection is a valid option to punish the wide opening range, calling is a prudent strategy to control the pot size while playing out of position. Calling keeps the opponent's range wide, including hands I dominate (lower pairs) or am flipping against (unpaired broadways), whereas a 3-bet might isolate me against the top of their range or invite a 4-bet that puts me in a difficult spot. Given the stack depth (85bb) and the early stage of the match, I will call to see a flop.

I hold Pocket Nines on a T-5-5 flop. Although there is an overcard (Ten) on the board, my hand is still quite strong in a heads-up scenario, beating lower pairs and floating overcards. However, being out of position, leading (donk betting) usually folds out the opponent's bluffs and isolates me against hands that beat me (Tx or trips). Checking is the standard play here to allow the pre-flop aggressor to make a continuation bet with their wide range, including flush draws and air. I intend to check-call a reasonable bet.

I have Queen-high on a Td-5d-5c board. My opponent checked to me after calling my pre-flop raise. This board is unlikely to have hit the opponent's calling range hard, unless they have a Ten or a Five. I will place a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to exert pressure and attempt to win the pot immediately. This represents strength and capitalizes on my position as the pre-flop aggressor.

I hold Pocket Nines on a Td-5d-5c flop. My opponent has continuation-bet 300 (half-pot). In a heads-up match, this board texture hits the pre-flop aggressor's range reasonably well (Tx, overpairs), but they will also be betting with flush draws, straight draws, and air. My hand is too strong to fold as it beats all bluffs and lower pairs (66-88). Raising would likely only get action from hands that beat me (Tx, 5x, JJ+) while folding out the bluffs I want to keep in the pot. Therefore, calling is the most profitable line to control the pot size while out of position and evaluate the turn.

I hold Pocket Nines on a Td-5d-5c-8h board. The turn card (8h) connects somewhat with the board but doesn't complete the obvious flush draw. I am out of position. My hand is a bluff-catcher at this point; I beat missed draws and smaller pairs (66, 77), but I lose to any Ten, trips (5x), or higher pairs. Leading out (donk betting) doesn't make much sense as it folds out bluffs and isolates me against better hands. Checking is the standard play to control the size of the pot and allow the opponent to potentially bluff or value bet worse hands. If the opponent checks back, I get a free look at the river. If they bet, I will re-evaluate based on sizing, though I likely have to call one more street given the heads-up width of ranges.

I currently have Queen-high on a board of Td-5d-5c-8h. I was the pre-flop aggressor and followed up with a continuation bet on the flop, which Pro called. Pro's check on the turn suggests he might be holding a marginal hand like a Ten, a small pocket pair, or a diamond draw. While a second barrel could potentially fold out some of his weaker holdings, checking back allows me to control the size of the pot and see a free river card. Queen-high still has some showdown value against missed draws, and checking behind keeps the pot manageable while I am out of position in terms of card strength.

I hold Pocket Nines on a final board of Td-5d-5c-8h-8c. My hand is effectively Two Pair (Nines and Eights) with a Ten kicker. The river card double-pairs the board, meaning my hand beats any unpaired hand (Ace-high) and lower pocket pairs (66, 77), but loses to any Ten, Eight, Five, or higher pocket pair. My opponent checked back the turn, which often indicates a draw that missed, a weak made hand (like a small pair or Ace-high), or pot control with a weak Ten. Leading out (betting) is unlikely to get value from worse hands, as Ace-high will fold, and better hands (Tx) will call. Checking allows me to get to showdown cheaply against his show-down value or potentially induce a bluff from his missed draws (diamonds). Given I have a bluff-catcher with showdown value, checking is the optimal line.

The board has double-paired (5s and 8s) on the river, making the final board Td-5d-5c-8h-8c. After Pro called my pre-flop raise and flop continuation bet, the turn and river went check-check. My Queen-high has some showdown value against missed diamond draws or lower cards like 76, but it loses to any Ten, any Eight, any Five, or any Ace-high. Given the way the hand played out, Pro likely has a marginal made hand like a Ten or a small pocket pair that has now become two pair (T-T-8-8 or 8-8-X-X). Since it is unlikely I can represent a hand strong enough to force a fold from a Ten or an Ace-high on this board without a massive overbet, checking back is the most prudent move to reach showdown for free.

In a Heads-Up match, J6o is a marginal hand (approximately top 56%). While it can be an open-raise from the Button (Small Blind) in a vacuum, the opponent 'Flash' has shown significant aggression, including a 3-bet in Hand #5 and raising nearly every button. Opening with a hand that plays poorly post-flop and cannot withstand a 3-bet risks bleeding chips unnecessarily. Folding is a disciplined, low-variance decision to preserve the stack for hands with better playability or high-card strength.

I am on the Button with Nine-Eight of spades, a strong suited connector that plays very well in heads-up situations. Given the previous hands, raising from the Small Blind has been my standard opening play to maintain aggression and leverage my positional advantage. A raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard sizing to build the pot while keeping the SPR manageable for post-flop play.

Opponent 'Flash' is opening frequently to 300 (3bb) from the button, indicating a wide range. J7 suited is a strong enough hand to defend the Big Blind heads-up, having roughly 40-45% equity against a typical wide opening range. I am getting 2:1 pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 600), requiring 33% equity to break even. Folding here would be too tight and exploitable. I will call to see a flop.

The flop is Ad-Ks-Ts. This texture heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor (Flash), as it connects well with broadway cards and Aces in their range. I hold Jd-7d, giving me a gutshot straight draw (needing a Queen) and a backdoor flush draw. While the gutshot is to the nuts, the presence of the flush draw on board and the likelihood that Flash has connected with this board (pair of Aces, Kings, two pair, or a straight already) makes this a dangerous spot. I am out of position. Leading out (donking) is not advisable as I will likely get raised or called by better. The standard play is to check and see what the opponent does. I will check with the intention of folding to a significant bet, as my equity is relatively low and implied odds are dampened by the flush potential.

I have a flush draw on a very high-card-heavy board (Ace, King, Ten). As the pre-flop aggressor, I want to continue my aggression with a continuation bet. Although the board favors a defending range that likely contains Broadway cards, betting here allows me to take down the pot if my opponent missed or is folding marginal hands. If called, I still have significant equity to improve to a flush. A half-pot bet of 300 is a standard sizing in this situation.

The flop [Ts Ks Ad] hits the opponent's opening range extremely hard, connecting with many Broadway hands, Aces, and Kings. I hold [Jd 7d], giving me a gutshot straight draw (needing a Queen) and a backdoor flush draw, but I currently only have Jack-high. The opponent bets 300 into a pot of 600, offering me 3:1 pot odds (25% equity required). With only 4 clean outs for the straight (roughly 8-9% equity on the next card) and negligible backdoor equity, I do not have the immediate odds to call. Furthermore, being out of position against a range advantage makes floating or bluffing unprofitable. The disciplined play is to fold.

I hold 9h 7c on the Button (SB). This is a 9-high offsuit one-gapper. While position is an advantage in heads-up play, 97o is a marginal hand that sits near the bottom of a playable range. In previous hands, I have folded hands like J6o and T5o, but raised with 76o (which has better connectivity). The opponent 'Flash' has shown aggression and a willingness to defend their Big Blind (e.g., Hand #7). Given the opponent's tendency to defend and my hand's lack of strong high-card value or premium connectivity, I will choose to fold to preserve my stack and avoid a potentially difficult post-flop situation with a weak hand.

K8o is a strong hand in heads-up play, significantly ahead of a random range. I have been raising to 300 from the button frequently, which has often resulted in the opponent folding or allowing me to take control of the pot. I will continue this aggressive strategy with a standard 3x raise.

I am in the Big Blind holding 8h 2c, which is a very weak hand (bottom 10% of hands). My opponent on the Button has raised to 300 (3bb). To call, I need to put in 200 chips into a pot that will be 600, requiring about 33% equity to break even. Against a standard heads-up opening range, 82o has poor equity and very poor playability out of position. The 3x raise size makes the price to call worse than a min-raise. Defending here would be -EV. I will fold.

In Heads-Up play, King-Six offsuit is a top 42% hand and standardly strong enough to open from the button. I have been folding my button frequently in recent hands (Hands 9, 11), so opening here is necessary to prevent the opponent from automatically profiting with any two cards. I have position post-flop. A min-raise to 200 is appropriate to apply pressure while minimizing loss if 3-bet, noting that the opponent successfully 3-bet me in Hand 5 when I held a similar hand (K2s).

I am in the Big Blind with 4c 3s. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 200, making it 100 more for me to call. While the pot odds are 3:1, 43o is one of the weakest possible starting hands and plays very poorly out of position post-flop. Given that I have a healthy chip lead and there are many hands left in the tournament, I will opt for a disciplined fold rather than defending with air.

I am on the Button (Small Blind) with Jc 7s. My opponent, Pro, has been playing quite tight, folding frequently to pre-flop raises. J7o is a hand I can comfortably open in a heads-up game to put pressure on a tight opponent and steal the blinds. I will continue my established pattern of raising to 3x the big blind.

I hold pocket sixes (6s 6h) in the Big Blind. My opponent, Flash, has raised to 300 (3bb) from the Button. Based on the history, Flash is opening very frequently from the button (almost 100% of observed hands). Pocket sixes are a strong enough hand to defend against a wide button opening range. 3-betting is an option, but it bloats the pot out of position with a hand that is difficult to play post-flop if overcards appear. Calling allows me to realize my equity, set-mine, or navigate a low board with a disguised hand. I am getting decent pot odds to call.

I hold pocket sixes on a [7d Kd 9s] board, giving me an underpair. This board texture favors the pre-flop aggressor who likely has Kings or draw equity in their range. Leading out (donk betting) serves little purpose as it folds out worse hands and isolates me against better ones. The standard line is to check to the raiser to see if they continue. I will check and re-evaluate if facing a bet, though folding to significant aggression is likely given my weak showdown value and lack of draws.

I have bottom pair (sevens) on a board with several potential draws (diamonds, straight draws like JT or T8). Pro has checked, and while my hand has some showdown value, it's vulnerable to many turn cards. By betting 300 (half-pot), I can deny equity to overcards and draws, while also getting information about the strength of Pro's hand. If I am raised, I can comfortably fold knowing I'm likely beaten by a King or a Nine.

I hold pocket sixes (6s 6h) on a flop of [7d Kd 9s]. This is a very poor board for my hand, as my pair is counterfeited by the board being entirely higher than my pocket pair (effectively 4th pair). The opponent, who has been aggressive, bets half-pot (300 into 600). The board texture (King high, connected mid-cards, flush draw) connects well with a wide Button raising range. I am likely behind any K, 9, 7, or higher pocket pair, and even against pure bluffs with overcards, I am vulnerable. I have very little equity and no profitable way to continue out of position. Folding is the correct decision.

I am on the button with T6s, a suited one-gapper. In heads-up play, this hand is strong enough to open-raise for value and to steal the blinds. I have position and a stack depth of 85bb. My opponent has shown aggression but also folds to aggression. A min-raise to 200 allows me to play a pot in position or take it down pre-flop.

Pro has consistently opened for 200 from the small blind. In Hand 5, I successfully 3-bet a similar open to 600 and forced a fold. Holding an Ace (As 2c) provides a significant blocker to many of Pro's strongest hands, and given my stack lead and Pro's previous tendency to fold to pre-flop aggression, a 3-bet to 600 is a high-frequency winning play here to take the pot down immediately or define the range while out of position.

I opened to 200 with T6s on the button, and the Big Blind 3-bet to 600. While T6s is a reasonable open in heads-up play, it is a marginal hand to defend against a 3-bet, even in position. I need to call 400 into a pot of 1200, requiring about 33% equity. Against a strong 3-betting range, T6s is often dominated and suffers from reverse implied odds (making dominated pairs or lower flushes). Although the opponent has been aggressive, I have not yet confirmed they are 3-betting light enough to make this a profitable call. Folding is the prudent play to preserve chips for better spots early in the match.

King-Ten suited is a premier hand in heads-up play, well above the average opening range. I will maintain my consistent opening size of 300 to exert pressure and build the pot. Given Pro's tendency to fold to aggression, this is a standard value raise.

The opponent raises to 300 (3bb) from the Button, consistent with their previous aggressive opening strategy. I hold Ten-Seven offsuit in the Big Blind. While heads-up play requires defending a wide range, facing a 3bb open makes the price to call steeper (requiring ~33% equity) compared to a min-raise. T7o has roughly 40-45% raw equity against a wide range, but realizing this equity out of position is difficult, especially against an opponent who has shown a tendency to c-bet aggressively (100% frequency in observed hands). T7o falls just outside the standard defense range against a 3x open. Preserving chips for better spots or better playability is the prudent choice here.

I am in the Small Blind (Button) with 98o, which is a standard opening hand in heads-up poker due to its connectivity and playability in position. Although the opponent has shown aggression with 3-bets against my previous opens (Hands 5 and 15), I cannot fold a hand this strong pre-flop, nor do I want to limp and concede initiative without a balanced limping strategy. A min-raise to 200 is the most standard and effective play to steal the blinds or build a pot where I have positional advantage.

I am in the Big Blind with 63o. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 2x (200). Although I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call, 63o is an extremely weak hand that performs poorly out of position and is difficult to realize equity with. Given my chip lead and the early stage of the match, I prefer to fold this bottom-tier hand rather than defend and potentially face difficult post-flop decisions.

I am on the button with Ace-five offsuit. This is a standard opening hand in heads-up play. Based on the previous hands, I have consistently raised to 3x (300 chips) from the button, and 'Pro' has shown a tendency to fold frequently pre-flop or early post-flop. I will maintain my aggressive strategy and raise to 300.

I hold 9d 2s in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise (300 chips) from the Button. This is a very weak off-suit hand. Although I am getting 3:1 pot odds, 92o has poor playability and realization of equity out of position. Against an aggressive opponent who opens frequently, I simply fold this trash hand and wait for a better spot.

I hold A9s on the Button, which is a premium hand in heads-up play. It has excellent equity and playability. My opponent has been aggressive, frequently 3-betting my opens, but this hand is strong enough to defend against a 3-bet or play well post-flop. A min-raise to 200 is my standard opening size to build the pot while maintaining flexibility.

I am in the Big Blind holding 5h 6s. Pro has raised to 200 from the Button. I need to call 100 more to see the flop in a pot that will be 400 total. This gives me 3:1 pot odds. While 56o is a weak speculative hand, in a heads-up situation, defending the Big Blind with any two connected cards is generally acceptable given the price. I will call to see the flop and evaluate based on board texture.

I have middle pair (Sixes) on a King-high board. As the defender in the Big Blind, checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard play here. I want to see how Pro reacts to the board. If Pro bets a reasonable amount, I have enough value with middle pair to call one street.

I hold As9s on a Kh8d6c board. As the pre-flop aggressor, I have a range advantage on this King-high board. However, the board texture connects somewhat with the Big Blind's calling range (pairs of 8s or 6s, straight draws like 75, 97). My hand has good showdown value as Ace-high, but little playability if I am check-raised, and my opponent has shown aggressive tendencies (check-raising or betting strongly). Betting folds out hands I am already beating (Queen-high, etc.) but gets called by any pair. Checking back allows me to realize my equity, keeps the pot small, and induces bluffs from inferior hands on later streets. It also protects my checking range. Given the dry nature of the board and my medium-strength showdown value, checking is the optimal play.

On the flop, Pro checked back after I checked, which suggests he likely doesn't have a King. The Turn 3s is a blank. I have middle pair (Sixes), which has some showdown value. I will check again to see if Pro wants to lead out; if he checks back again, it strengthens the likelihood that my pair of sixes is currently the best hand, and it allows me to control the pot size with a marginal hand.

I hold As9s on a K-8-6-3 board. The turn card (3s) is a brick that doesn't change the board texture significantly or improve my hand beyond adding a single spade (irrelevant for a flush now). My opponent has checked to me again. I have good showdown value with Ace-high. Betting here serves little purpose: better hands (pairs) will likely call, and worse hands (worse high cards) will fold, meaning I don't extract value or bluff effectively. Checking back allows me to realize my equity, control the pot size, and hopefully get to a cheap showdown or induce a bluff on the river.

I have Two Pair, Eights and Sixes (8-8-6-6-K), which is a strong hand in a heads-up pot that has been checked all the way to the river. The Eight on the river paired the board, which technically improved my hand from a single pair of Sixes to two pair. Pro has checked the flop and turn, making it unlikely he holds a King or a pocket pair like AA-99, as those would typically bet for value. However, an 8 is possible, though he might have bet it on the turn. I will check to see if Pro wants to bet a missed draw or Ace-high, or simply to take the hand to a free showdown and realize my value.

On the River, the board is K-8-6-3-8. I hold As9s (Ace-high). The action has gone check-check on the flop and turn. My opponent checks to me again. I have showdown value beating all missed straight draws (like 75, 97, T9) and worse high cards. Betting is unlikely to get value from worse hands, and bluffing is risky as my line (checking back two streets) doesn't strongly represent a King or an 8, so small pairs might look me up. A raise to 300 is consistent with my previous betting patterns and allows me to take control of the hand pre-flop.

Opponent opens to 300 (3BB) from the Button/SB. I hold 4c3c in the Big Blind with a stack of 2900 (29BB). While 43s is a suited connector, it is the lowest possible one. Facing a 3x open, my pot odds are 2:1, requiring ~33% equity to call. While raw equity against a wide range is close to this, realizing that equity Out of Position with a relatively short stack is difficult. The 3x sizing puts extra pressure on my stack compared to a min-raise. Defending here is likely -EV or marginal at best. I choose to fold and preserve my chips for better spots (like high cards or medium connectors) to counter his frequent aggression.

I hold 7h3h in the Small Blind. This is a weak hand (bottom 25% of hands), even in heads-up play. With a stack of 28.5 BB compared to my opponent's 170 BB, I need to choose my spots to open-raise carefully. While I have position post-flop if called, this hand has poor playability and high reverse implied odds. Folding is the standard and disciplined play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with 96o. Pro is the Big Blind with a relatively short stack of 27.5 BB. I have been consistently raising to 300 from the button to put pressure on Pro, who has been folding frequently to pre-flop aggression. While 96o is marginal, it is a standard raise in heads-up play to maintain initiative and exploit Pro's high fold-to-steal frequency.

The opponent has raised to 300 (3bb) from the button, which has been their consistent sizing. I am in the Big Blind with J8o and a stack of 27.5bb. Facing a 3x raise, my pot odds are 33% (calling 200 to win a total pot of 600). While J8o is a reasonable hand in a vacuum heads-up, playing it out of position against a larger raise size significantly reduces my ability to realize equity. Standard heads-up ranges typically fold J8o against a 3bb open at this stack depth, as it falls just below the profitable defense threshold compared to defending against a min-raise. To conserve chips for better spots or where I have position/initiative, I will fold.

I am on the Button with 27 big blinds, holding Jack-Ten offsuit. This is a strong enough hand to open for a raise in heads-up play, as it has good high-card strength and playability. My standard opening size has been a min-raise to 200, which puts pressure on the Big Blind without committing too many chips if 3-bet. I will raise to 200 to try to steal the blinds or play the pot in position.

I am holding Ten-Eight of hearts in the big blind. Pro, who is on a short stack of approximately 25.5 big blinds, has min-opened from the button. I am getting 3:1 pot odds to make the call. While this hand has decent potential to flop well, I will choose to call rather than 3-bet to avoid potentially facing a shove and to keep the pot manageable while I have the chip lead.

On this Ace-high paired board of 4-4-A, the pre-flop raiser (Pro) has a significant range advantage as they are more likely to hold an Ace. My hand, Ten-Eight of hearts, is currently just air with a backdoor flush draw. As the big blind defender, I should check to the aggressor to see how they react to this board. This allows me to see a turn cheaply if they check back or to evaluate their bet size if they decide to continuation bet.

The board texture (A-4-4) is very dry and favors my range as the pre-flop raiser, but my specific hand (Jack-high) has only marginal showdown value. While a c-bet could fold out better high cards (like King-high or Queen-high), it would also fold out many hands I am currently beating. Conversely, if I bet and get called, I am almost certainly behind and drawing thin. Given my stack depth (25 BBs), exercising pot control by checking back is a prudent play. It allows me to realize equity for free, potentially improve on the turn, or reach showdown cheaply against the opponent's missed hands. I have taken similar checking lines on Ace-high boards in recent hands (e.g., Hand #81).

On the turn 3d, the board remains Ace-high and paired. Pro checked back the flop, which is unusual for the pre-flop raiser on such a dry board unless they are either trapping with a very strong hand (Ax or a 4) or have completely given up. With only Ten-high and no real draw, I have no showdown value. However, leading into the pre-flop raiser here is risky given their short stack, as they may be looking to induve a bluff or might simply call with any pocket pair. I will check again to see if they check back, allowing me to potentially see a river for free or evaluate their action if they decide to bet.

On the turn, the board reads 4d-Ac-4h-3d. My opponent checks again, showing continued weakness or pot control. With Jack-high, I have marginal showdown value but am vulnerable to King-high or Queen-high hands. Betting here as a bluff (delayed c-bet) could fold out those better high-card hands, but it risks chips against a sticky opponent or a trap (like a slow-played Ace or 4). Given my stack size (around 25 BB), checking back allows me to realize my equity for free (hitting a Jack or Ten on the river) and keeps the pot manageable. I can re-evaluate on the river depending on the card and opponent's action.

On the river, the board is 4-A-4-3-6 and the action has been checked through on both the flop and the turn. I am holding Ten-high, which has almost no showdown value. However, since the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) checked back twice on such a dry board, they likely have a weak hand themselves, such as a low pocket pair or King/Queen high. While a bluff might occasionally work, the pot is small (400) and I have a significant chip lead. I will check and hope to see a cheap showdown or fold if they decide to bet, as I don't want to commit more chips to a pot where I have no value.

I arrive at the river with Jack-high after the action checked through on the flop and turn. The board reads 4-A-4-3-6. My hand has very little showdown value, beating only missed straight draws (like 7-5 or T-9) or worse high cards. I lose to any pair, King-high, and Queen-high. While betting might fold out King-high or Queen-high hands, it is unlikely to fold out any pair (like a 3 or 6) from the chip leader who may be checking to induce or pot-controlling. Furthermore, my line of checking twice makes representing a strong Ace less credible. With a stack of ~25BB, preserving chips is prioritised over a marginal bluff. Checking back guarantees I realise my equity (however small) without risking chips in a spot where I am often looked up.

I am currently the chip leader with a significant advantage. While 7-2 offsuit is the weakest starting hand in poker, the strategy against a short-stacked opponent (Pro has less than 30 BB) involves maintaining pressure. Pro has shown a tendency to fold to pre-flop raises recently. I will put in my standard raise of 300 to attempt to win the blinds immediately. If Pro decides to shove, I can comfortably fold without damaging my stack, but the fold equity here makes the raise worthwhile.

I hold AQs, a premium hand in heads-up play. My opponent Flash has opened to 3bb (300). I have 28.5 big blinds (2850 chips). Given the stack depth, this is an excellent spot to 3-bet shove. AQs dominates the opponent's wide opening range. I have successfully 3-bet jammed over Flash's 300 opens in recent hands (e.g., Hand #90 with AJo, Hand #72 with 88) and generated folds. Flat calling is passive and lets me play out of position; a small 3-bet commits a significant portion of my stack awkwardly. Shoving maximizes fold equity and realizes my hand's strong equity if called.

Pro has effectively shoved by raising to 2850 with only 100 chips left in their stack. My initial raise to 300 with 7-2 offsuit was an attempt to exploit Pro's recent tendency to fold, but since Pro has responded with a massive 3-bet, I must acknowledge that my hand is the worst possible starting hand in poker. To call 2550 more, I would need roughly 44% equity against Pro's range to break even. Against even a wide shoving range, 7-2o rarely has more than 30-35% equity, and against a standard value range, it has significantly less. Despite my massive chip lead, there is no strategic reason to gift Pro a double-up with a sub-optimal hand. I will preserve my stack and wait for a better spot to utilize my chip advantage.

I am in the Small Blind with 5d 3c, which is a very weak hand (53 offsuit). My stack is 3200 (32 BBs), and my opponent has a significant chip lead. Playing this hand out of position is negative EV. The standard play is to fold.

I have 75 suited on the button. This is a standard opening hand in heads-up play, especially given my significant chip lead over Pro, who has about 31 big blinds remaining. A 3x raise has been my consistent sizing and is effective for applying pressure while remaining foldable if the opponent decides to shove.

I am in the Big Blind with K9o and facing a 3x raise (300) from the Button/Small Blind. My opponent has a large chip lead, and I am sitting on 31 BBs. K9o is a respectable hand heads-up, with roughly 50-55% equity against a typical wide button opening range. The pot odds are favorable: I need to call 200 to win a pot of 400, requiring 33% equity. A 3-bet shove is too risky at this stack depth (31 BBs) as it risks my tournament life for a small pot and gets called primarily by dominating hands. Folding would be too tight given the price and hand strength. Therefore, the correct play is to call and defend the Big Blind.

I am out of position on the Flop with King-high (K9o) on a J-6-4 two-tone board. The board texture favors the pre-flop aggressor's range, which likely includes Jacks, overpairs, or flush draws. Leading out (donk betting) serves little purpose here as I have no strong draw or made hand to balance it with. Checking is the standard play to see what the opponent does. If they bet, I will evaluate based on sizing, but my default posture is to check-fold unless the bet is very small, as I have limited equity and a shallow stack.

I have 75 suited on a 4s Jd 6s board, giving me an inside straight draw. The board has two spades, and while I don't have a flush draw, I have some equity. Pro checked to me after calling pre-flop. A continuation bet of about half the pot (300) is standard here to apply pressure on his marginal holdings and to potentially take down the pot immediately, while keeping the door open to improve on the turn.

I hold K9o on a J-6-4 two-tone board. After checking, my opponent bets 300 (half pot). While I have two overcards to the middle and bottom pair, I have no flush or straight draws. A half-pot bet on this wet texture often indicates either a made hand, a strong draw, or a c-bet intended to fold out equity like mine. Without a spade or better backdoor potential, my hand is too weak to continue out of position. Calling would bleed chips from my 29bb stack with very low playability on future streets. The disciplined play is to fold.

I am in the Small Blind (Button) with 28.5 BBs. My hand, 85o, is statistically very weak (bottom 25% of hands). Against a massive chip leader who has been active, entering the pot with this holding is -EV. While I have position post-flop, the hand has poor playability and cannot withstand a 3-bet. Folding is the standard play to preserve the stack for better opportunities.

I am on the Button with King-Nine offsuit, which is a strong hand in heads-up play. My opponent, Pro, is relatively short-stacked with 27.5 big blinds. I will continue with my standard opening raise to 300 to exert pressure and potentially win the blinds or see a flop in a position of strength.

I am in the Big Blind with 9s 2c, which is statistically one of the weakest starting hands in heads-up play. My opponent, who holds a significant chip lead, has opened for 3BB (300 chips). With a stack of 27.5 BBs, I need to be selective about the pots I contest. Calling requires investing 200 chips with a hand that has poor playability and low equity realization, especially out of position against a 3x open. There is no strategic merit in defending this specific hand or attempting a bluff re-raise here. Conserving my stack for better opportunities is the optimal play.

I am on the button with T9o and 27BB. This is a playable hand in heads-up poker, often strong enough to steal the blinds or play in position. My opponent has a large chip lead, but I cannot afford to play too passively. Raising to 200 puts pressure on the BB and allows me to define the pot size. If faced with a 3-bet, I can easily fold. Limping might invite a raise which puts me in a difficult spot out of position post-flop (if I were BB) or just facing aggression. Raising is the standard aggressive action here.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Nine offsuit (A9o), which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. The opponent (Pro) is short-stacked with only 25.5 BBs remaining and has a consistent pattern of min-raising to 200 and then folding to a 3-bet of 600 (as seen in Hands #5, #15, #89, and #93). Given this history, I will put in a 3-bet to 600. This size has consistently won the pot pre-flop against Pro's opening raises and maximizes the pressure on their dwindling stack.

I opened to 200 with T9o on the button, and the opponent in the Big Blind 3-bet to 600. While I have position, my hand is unsuited and relatively weak against a 3-bet range. My stack is 25.5 BBs, and calling would create a pot of 1200 with 2150 behind (SPR ~1.8), putting me in a high-variance spot with a marginal hand. Given the opponent's chip lead and previous aggression, waiting for a better spot to commit chips is prudent. Pot odds of 33% are not compelling enough for T9o here.

I am in the Small Blind with QhTd and a significant stack lead. My opponent, Pro, is very short-stacked with only 2,450 chips (24.5 big blinds). Looking at the history, Pro has been playing relatively tight-aggressive but has also shown a willingness to fold to 3-bets or shove over raises when short. Given the stack depths, I want to apply maximum pressure. Raising to 300 is a standard open that forces Pro to either commit a large portion of their stack to see a flop or fold. Since QhTd is well ahead of a random range and Pro has been folding to many pre-flop raises lately (Hand #102, #106, #107), a raise here is highly profitable.

I am in the Big Blind with [4d 4c] and a stack of 24.5 BBs. The opponent (Flash) has raised to 300 (3 BBs). A pocket pair of 4s is a respectable hand heads-up but vulnerable to overcards. Shoving all-in for 24.5 BBs is a viable exploit if the opponent folds often, but it is high variance and slightly over-aggressive against a 3x open where I am often flipping or dominated if called. Calling allows me to see a flop cheaply (investing 200 more to win a pot of 600) with good implied odds if I hit a set (1 in 8 chance). I'm getting appropriate pot odds to defend. I will call and re-evaluate on the flop.

I hold pocket fours on a [7c Tc 3h] flop. I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser. My hand is an underpair to the board and vulnerable to many overcards and draws. Leading out (donk betting) is generally poor strategy here as it folds out worse hands (bluffs) and gets called by better. The standard line is to check to the aggressor to see if they continue or give a free card. I will check and evaluate based on their action.

I have flopped top pair with a Queen kicker. The board is [7c, Tc, 3h], which is relatively safe but contains a club flush draw. Pro has checked to me. Given Pro's short stack (2250 chips remaining), I need to bet for value and to protect my hand against potential draws. A bet of 300 into the 600 pot (50% pot) is a standard sizing that builds the pot while allowing me to easily commit the rest of the chips on later streets if the board remains favorable.

I hold pocket fours (4d 4c) on a flop of 7c Tc 3h. I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser who has bet 300 into a pot of 600. My hand is an underpair to two cards on the board and vulnerable to many turn cards (overcards, clubs, straights). In Hand #14, I folded 66 on a 7-K-9 board in a similar spot. Calling would require investing ~13% of my remaining stack with a hand that is often behind or flipping against draws, with poor playability on future streets. Preserving my 22.5 BB stack for a better spot is the prudent long-term play.

I am in the Small Blind (Button) with 22BBs, holding T4o, which is a very weak hand (offsuit, disconnected, low kicker). Given the significant stack disadvantage (Flash has ~176BBs), I need to be selective with my opening hands to preserve my stack for better opportunities. T4o performs poorly post-flop and cannot withstand a 3-bet. Folding is the standard and correct play here to minimize losses.

I hold KTo on the button, which is a strong hand in heads-up play. I have a massive chip lead over Pro, who only has 21 big blinds remaining. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to apply pressure, leverage my positional advantage, and attempt to take down the pot pre-flop or control the hand on later streets.

I am in the Big Blind with K7s, holding 21 BBs (2100 chips). The opponent (Flash) on the Button has opened to 300 (3BB). K7s is a strong hand in heads-up play, possessing good equity against a wide button opening range and decent playability post-flop. With 21 BBs, a 3-bet shove is a consideration but slightly large; flat calling is the standard low-variance play to realize equity. The price is 200 to call into a pot of 400 (2:1 odds), which is profitable for this hand. I will call to see a flop.

I hold King-high with a backdoor flush draw on a J-9-3 board. Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check to the raiser. I do not have a strong enough hand to lead for value, and leading as a bluff is unlikely to be profitable given the board texture connects with the opponent's range. I will check and evaluate based on their action.

On a flop of J-9-3, I have two overcards and a gutshot straight draw to the nuts. Pro has checked to me after calling pre-flop. With Pro's short stack of 1,900 chips, I want to maintain aggression and apply pressure. A continuation bet here often takes down the pot immediately against his air and puts him in a difficult spot with marginal hands. If he shoves, I have enough equity with my overcards and draw to consider a call, but the primary goal is to fold out his equity now.

I hold King-high (Ks 7s) on a board of 3d-9h-Js. The opponent has c-bet 300 into a pot of 600 (half-pot). I have no immediate draws, only weak backdoor straight and flush possibilities. With 19 BBs remaining, I cannot afford to float out of position with such low equity against a standard c-bet range that likely connects with this board (J-x, 9-x, straight draws) or dominates my high card. Preserving my stack for a better pre-flop shove or clearer post-flop situation is the optimal strategy.

I am on the Button (SB) with a short stack of 18.5 BBs (1850 chips) against a massive chip leader. I hold 4d 2s, which is one of the worst starting hands in Hold'em (4-2 offsuit). Given my stack depth, I cannot afford to bleed chips with such low equity hands. Limping is risky as the aggressive opponent likely raises, and raising myself is lighting chips on fire. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve the stack for a better spot.

I am on the button with 98o. My opponent, Pro, is very short-stacked with only 1,750 chips (17.5 BB). Historically, Pro has been folding to my 3x pre-flop raises the vast majority of the time. Given my massive chip lead and the high fold equity I have against a player trying to survive until the end of the 150 hands, raising to 300 is a consistent and effective play to pick up the blinds.

I hold Pocket Fours (4c 4h) in the Big Blind with a stack of 1,750 chips (17.5 BBs). The opponent on the Button has raised to 300 (3 BBs). With a low pocket pair, I have a made hand that is ahead of the opponent's random opening range but vulnerable to overcards. Shoving 17.5 BBs is high variance as I am likely flipping or dominated if called, and the 3x sizing makes the risk/reward ratio of a shove slightly less attractive than against a min-raise. Calling requires 200 chips to win a pot that will be 600, giving me 2:1 odds. This allows me to see a flop; I can continue aggressively on low boards or hit a set, while retaining the option to fold on dangerous high-card textures without risking my tournament life immediately. I choose to defend by calling.

The flop comes Ad-3h-Qc, which hits the pre-flop raiser's range very hard (plenty of Ax and Qx combos). I hold pocket fours, which is now a weak underpair. I am out of position with a shallow stack of 15.5 BBs. There is no merit in leading out (donk betting) as I will only be called by better hands or raised. My best course of action is to check. If the opponent bets, I will likely have to fold given the board texture strongly favors their range and I have minimal equity. If they check back, I can re-evaluate on the turn.

I have 9-high on a board of A-Q-3 after raising pre-flop. Pro has checked to me. Given Pro's short stack and tight playing style throughout the match, a standard continuation bet should be effective here. Even though the board contains two high cards that could hit a defending range, Pro's tendency to fold under pressure makes a half-pot bet a high-probability play to take down the pot immediately without risking much of my significant chip lead.

The flop Ad-3h-Qc interacts very poorly with my hand (Pocket Fours) and very well with the opponent's raising range (Ax, Qx, Broadways). Facing a half-pot c-bet of 300 into 600, I am getting 3:1 odds, but my equity is likely extremely low. I am drawing to 2 outs against any top or middle pair, and even against pure bluffs, I am vulnerable to overcards on the turn and river. With a stack of 15.5 BBs, I cannot afford to call off nearly 20% of my remaining chips with a weak underpair out of position on a board that heavily favors the aggressor. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the Button (Small Blind) with 15BB and KTo. This is a strong hand in heads-up play. Open-shoving 15BB is a viable option to deny equity and prevent being 3-bet bluffed, but it risks little to win little. A min-raise to 200 is the standard play at this stack depth, allowing me to play a pot in position against a wider range that likely includes hands I dominate (like K9, Qx, Jx). If the opponent 3-bets, I can re-evaluate, but KTo is strong enough to defend or shove over a 3-bet given the stack sizes. I will make a standard opening raise.

I am holding 6c 3h in the big blind. Pro, who is very short-stacked with only 13.5 BB, has raised to 200 from the small blind. This is a rare show of aggression from Pro recently. I need to commit only 100 more chips to see the flop, which gives me 3:1 pot odds. In a heads-up situation, 63o has enough equity (roughly 30-35% against most reasonable ranges) to justify a call at these odds. Additionally, with my significant chip lead, I can afford to see a flop and potentially outplay Pro post-flop if he continues his 'fit-or-fold' pattern, or simply fold if the board does not improve my hand.

On this King-high flop, Pro, the pre-flop aggressor, has checked. My 6-high hand has no value and very little equity, but in a heads-up pot where the opponent is very short-stacked and playing tight, a check here is the most standard play. I will check behind and see if I can improve on the turn or if Pro's action on the next street gives me more information about his hand strength.

I hold Top Pair (Kings) with a Ten kicker on a relatively dry board (K-9-5 rainbow). My stack is short (13.5 BB), and the SPR is roughly 3.4. While betting for value is a standard option, checking back in position has significant merits: 1) It disguises the strength of my hand, protecting my checking range. 2) It induces the opponent (Flash) to bluff on the turn or river with weaker hands that would fold to a flop bet. 3) It exercises pot control, avoiding a difficult spot if I get check-raised. Given the dry texture, the risk of giving a free card is minimal compared to the value of inducing action from a wide defensive range. I plan to call bets on future streets or bet for value if checked to again.

I am currently holding six-high with no showdown value or significant draws. Pro has checked for the second time on a board of King-high. While Pro's passivity suggests they may have missed this board, their stack is very short, and they have shown a tendency to play extremely 'fit-or-fold.' Checking behind is the most prudent move here, as it allows me to see the river for free without risking chips on a pure bluff against a short stack who might be induced to call with any marginal piece of the board.

I hold Top Pair (Kings) and previously checked the flop for pot control and deception. Flash has checked to me again on the turn, signaling a likely capped range consisting of weak pairs or air. The 2h introduces a flush draw, so giving another free card carries more risk than on the flop. As per my previous plan, I will now bet for value to extract chips from 9x, 5x, and smaller pocket pairs, and to protect against the flush draw. A half-pot bet of 200 is sized to get called by worse hands while leaving room to maneuver on the river.

Pro has been playing an incredibly tight strategy, exemplified by folding pocket Jacks pre-flop in Hand #111. On this King-high board, Pro checked the flop but has now led for a 200-chip bet on the turn. Although I am getting 4:1 pot odds, my hand (6-high) has absolutely no showdown value and no draws. Given Pro's extremely risk-averse tendencies, a bet from them—even a small one—usually indicates they have at least a marginal piece of the board or an Ace. There is no reason to continue with 6-high; folding is the correct and disciplined play here to preserve my massive chip lead.

I am holding pocket Kings, the second-best starting hand in Hold'em. My opponent, Pro, is very short-stacked with only 1,650 chips (16.5 BBs). My goal is to maximize value and potentially eliminate the opponent. I will make my standard 3x raise to 300 to build the pot and encourage a mistake or an all-in from a wide range of hands that Pro might defend with.

I am heads-up with 16.5 big blinds (1650 chips) in the Big Blind. The opponent, Flash, has a massive chip lead and has been opening to 300 (3BB) frequently, indicating a very wide range. I hold King-Nine offsuit (Ks 9c), which is a strong hand heads-up but difficult to play post-flop out of position, especially against an aggressive opponent who c-bets often (as seen in Hand 104, 108, 110, 112 where I called and folded to c-bets). Calling and folding on the flop is slowly bleeding my stack. A reshove (all-in) puts maximum pressure on the opponent, leveraging fold equity to take down the 400 chip pot (increasing my stack by ~25% without a showdown) or realizing my equity fully if called. Given Flash's wide opening frequency, he likely holds many hands that cannot call a shove. K9o blocks King-high calling hands and has reasonable equity against a calling range.

I have pocket Kings, the second-best starting hand. My opponent, Pro, has raised to 1,650, leaving himself with only 100 chips behind. Given his extremely short stack and the strength of my hand, I will raise to 1,750 to put him all-in and maximize my chance of eliminating him or taking his remaining chips.

I have 100 chips remaining in my stack and am facing a raise to 1750 after putting in 1650. The pot is currently 3400. I need to call 100 more to contest the pot. This gives me pot odds of 34:1. With King-Nine offsuit, I have significant equity against any two cards, and even if dominated, the price is too good to fold. Furthermore, folding would leave me with only 1 big blind, which is a disastrous position. This is a mandatory call to close the action and realize my equity.
